chapters.title: The Low-Stakes Live Cash Operating System
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is to play a value-first, evidence-led strategy instead of trying to win every interesting pot. In plain English: start with default to patience, position, value betting, and disciplined folds until a specific opponent proves the pool tendency is wrong. and protect yourself from treating low-stakes live poker like a creativity contest when it is usually a mistake-harvesting contest.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of expected value, population tendencies, patience, selective aggression. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Low-stakes live pools give away money through repetition. They call too many weak hands, overvalue one-pair holdings, underbluff the most expensive nodes, and let impatience create pots that did not need to exist. A value-first operating system works because it turns those repeated mistakes into a stable routine instead of forcing the player to invent a new personality every hand.
takeaways: Default to patience, position, value betting, and disciplined folds until a specific opponent proves the pool tendency is wrong.
Avoid this leak: Treating low-stakes live poker like a creativity contest when it is usually a mistake-harvesting contest.
Ask before acting: Before entering a pot, ask: what mistake am I trying to get paid by, and will this hand realize that edge?
what_to_do_first: Play a value-first exploitative default. At typical $1/$2 and $1/$3 tables, assume opponents call too much before you assume they fold too much.
Make money with boring repetition. Table select, enter pots with position or clear value, value bet when worse hands call, and fold when passive lines become large and polarized.
Do not pay the curiosity tax. If the only reason to call is wanting to see it, treat that as a fold trigger unless the player has already shown real bluff frequency.
Use bluffs selectively. Bluff capped ranges and players capable of folding; do not bluff the player whose main leak is calling.

decision_questions.question: What is my default style?
decision_questions.answer: Play tight enough to avoid dominated trouble, aggressive enough to charge mistakes, and patient enough to let weaker players overpay when you have value.

decision_questions.question: When do I stop being patient?
decision_questions.answer: When you have position, a clear value target, a capped opponent, or a player-specific read that creates fold equity. Patience is not passivity; it is selectivity.

decision_questions.question: What should I ignore?
decision_questions.answer: Ignore the urge to match the table's splashiness. Loose games reward disciplined value extraction more than loose imitation.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Default to patience, position, value betting, and disciplined folds until a specific opponent proves the pool tendency is wrong.
Trigger question: Before entering a pot, ask: what mistake am I trying to get paid by, and will this hand realize that edge?
Play a value-first exploitative default. At typical $1/$2 and $1/$3 tables, assume opponents call too much before you assume they fold too much.
Make money with boring repetition. Table select, enter pots with position or clear value, value bet when worse hands call, and fold when passive lines become large and polarized.
Do not pay the curiosity tax. If the only reason to call is wanting to see it, treat that as a fold trigger unless the player has already shown real bluff frequency.
advanced_notes: The boring line is often the highest-EV line.

Low-stakes live players tend to reward patience and punish ego. Passing on marginal spots can feel like leaving money behind, but the money usually appears when weaker players overcall value, overplay medium strength, or underbluff large pots.

chapters.title: Preflop Discipline and Seat Selection
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that preflop profit starts before cards are dealt: choose better seats, enter fewer dominated spots, and attack limpers with position. In plain English: start with sit with loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right; open tighter up front; widen late only when position and table texture let you realize equity. and protect yourself from using a loose table as permission to play loose hands out of position.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of position, stack depth, table selection, isolation raising. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Preflop is where low-stakes players quietly donate future-street EV. Loose tables tempt players into dominated calls, weak offsuit broadways, speculative hands without implied odds, and out-of-position spots that look cheap until top pair becomes expensive. Seat selection compounds the effect because acting after the splashiest stacks lets you collect information before committing money.
takeaways: Sit with loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right; open tighter up front; widen late only when position and table texture let you realize equity.
Avoid this leak: Using a loose table as permission to play loose hands out of position.
Ask before acting: Before calling or raising preflop, ask: will this hand make clean value, win with position, or trap me into second-best one-pair hands?
what_to_do_first: Best seat-selection default: put loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right so you act after them more often; avoid strong aggressive players on your immediate left because they can 3-bet and squeeze your opens.
Early position starter range: open roughly 66+, AJs+, KQs, AQo+; add hands like AJo or KQo only when the table behind is passive and unlikely to punish dominated top-pair spots.
Middle position starter range: add 55+, ATs+, KJs+, QJs, JTs, KQo, and suited broadways that can make strong top pair or nut draws without creating too many reverse-implied-odds problems.
Cutoff/button default: widen when blinds are passive: add most pairs, suited aces, suited broadways, suited connectors around 65s+, and offsuit broadways that dominate blind-calling ranges.

Versus limpers: isolate to about 4-5bb plus 1bb per limper in position with hands ahead of their limp-calling range; overlimp small pairs and suited connectors only when stacks are deep and several players are likely to pay off.
Avoid the classic leak: do not cold-call dominated offsuit broadways out of position just because the table is loose.

decision_questions.question: Where should I sit?
decision_questions.answer: Prefer the seat with loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right and fewer strong aggressive players on your left. Acting after the weakest money is a recurring edge.

decision_questions.question: What should I open?
decision_questions.answer: Use a tight live-cash starter map: early position roughly 66+, AJs+, KQs, AQo+; middle position adds suited broadways, ATs+, KJs+, QJs, JTs, 55+ and KQo; cutoff and button widen when the blinds are passive and stacks let you realize equity.

decision_questions.question: When should I isolate limpers?
decision_questions.answer: Isolate to about 4-5bb plus 1bb per limper when you are in position, ahead of the limp-calling range, and deep enough to get paid. Overlimp speculative hands only when the table will pay off big made hands.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Sit with loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right; open tighter up front; widen late only when position and table texture let you realize equity.
Trigger question: Before calling or raising preflop, ask: will this hand make clean value, win with position, or trap me into second-best one-pair hands?

Best seat-selection default: put loose, deep, mistake-prone players on your right so you act after them more often; avoid strong aggressive players on your immediate left because they can 3-bet and squeeze your opens.
Early position starter range: open roughly 66+, AJs+, KQs, AQo+; add hands like AJo or KQo only when the table behind is passive and unlikely to punish dominated top-pair spots.
Middle position starter range: add 55+, ATs+, KJs+, QJs, JTs, KQo, and suited broadways that can make strong top pair or nut draws without creating too many reverse-implied-odds problems.
advanced_notes: The best preflop decision may happen before you look at your cards.
Changing seats, refusing dominated hands, and declining bad-position calls can be worth more than a clever postflop line. It feels less heroic because it prevents hard spots instead of solving them.

chapters.title: Position, Initiative, and Decision Leverage
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that position and initiative are not slogans; they are tools for making later streets easier and more profitable. In plain English: start with prefer hands and lines that let you act last, keep range advantage credible, and know which turn cards continue the story before you bet the flop. and protect yourself from taking the betting lead without a turn plan, then feeling forced to continue after the board or opponent says stop.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of position, initiative, range advantage, future street leverage. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Position works in live poker because most opponents reveal more than they intend. They check when uncomfortable, call when curious, and raise more honestly than balanced theory would require. Acting last lets you use those signals before building the pot.
takeaways: Prefer hands and lines that let you act last, keep range advantage credible, and know which turn cards continue the story before you bet the flop.
Avoid this leak: Taking the betting lead without a turn plan, then feeling forced to continue after the board or opponent says stop.
Ask before acting: Before betting, ask: does this action buy clearer future decisions or only preserve momentum?
what_to_do_first: Prefer position over marginal hand strength. A slightly weaker hand in position often earns more than a prettier hand out of position.
Use initiative to simplify. Continuation bet more often heads-up on dry high-card boards; check more often multiway, on coordinated boards, or when your range loses credibility.
Plan the turn before betting flop. If you do not know which turn cards let you continue, the flop bet may be autopilot.
Apply pressure where future streets hurt villain. Position lets you threaten turn and river bets when villain's range is capped.

decision_questions.question: Is position worth more than a pretty hand?
decision_questions.answer: Often yes. A marginal hand in position can outperform a prettier hand out of position because it captures information and controls pot growth.

decision_questions.question: When should I continuation bet?
decision_questions.answer: Bet more often heads-up on boards that favor your range; slow down multiway, on coordinated textures, or when you lack a credible turn plan.

decision_questions.question: How do I use initiative without spewing?
decision_questions.answer: Decide which turns continue before you bet the flop. If most turns leave you guessing, the flop bet is probably autopilot.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Prefer hands and lines that let you act last, keep range advantage credible, and know which turn cards continue the story before you bet the flop.
Trigger question: Before betting, ask: does this action buy clearer future decisions or only preserve momentum?
Prefer position over marginal hand strength. A slightly weaker hand in position often earns more than a prettier hand out of position.
Use initiative to simplify. Continuation bet more often heads-up on dry high-card boards; check more often multiway, on coordinated boards, or when your range loses credibility.
Plan the turn before betting flop. If you do not know which turn cards let you continue, the flop bet may be autopilot.
advanced_notes: A weaker hand in position can be more profitable than a prettier hand out of position.
The hidden value is not the cards themselves. It is the ability to see one more decision, control the pot, and apply pressure when the opponent's range becomes capped.

chapters.title: Range Reading with Population Shortcuts
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is to start from live-pool priors, then narrow ranges street by street instead of giving every opponent every hand forever. In plain English: start with assume low-stakes players call too wide, raise too honestly, and underbluff big passive lines until their history proves otherwise. and protect yourself from inventing balanced ranges for opponents who are using ordinary live-player incentives.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of range construction, capped ranges, line consistency, population reads. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Population shortcuts work because most low-stakes opponents are not constructing protected ranges. Their calls remain too wide, their raises remain too value-heavy, and their river aggression often contains fewer bluffs than the price requires. The shortcut is a starting prior, not a stereotype.
takeaways: Assume low-stakes players call too wide, raise too honestly, and underbluff big passive lines until their history proves otherwise.
Avoid this leak: Inventing balanced ranges for opponents who are using ordinary live-player incentives.
Ask before acting: Before continuing, ask: what hands are actually still consistent with this action, sizing, and player type?
what_to_do_first: Start with the population prior, then update. Low-stakes players call too wide, raise too honestly, and underbluff big river lines until proven otherwise.
Remove hands street by street. Do not keep weak floats, missed draws, or slowplays in range after later actions make them unlikely.
Treat passive raises as value-heavy. When a passive player raises turn or river, require a specific reason before assigning many bluffs.
Respect line consistency. A story that makes no sense can be bluffing, but at low stakes it is often confusion plus value; use sizing and player type to decide.

decision_questions.question: What population read should I start with?
decision_questions.answer: Start with calls too wide, raises too honest, and big passive river lines underbluffed. Then update only from observed behavior.

decision_questions.question: How do I narrow a range?
decision_questions.answer: Remove hands that no longer fit each action. A hand that might call flop may not raise turn or overbet river at meaningful frequency.

decision_questions.question: When do I stop trusting the pool read?
decision_questions.answer: When the player has shown repeated behavior that contradicts it: real bluffs, merged raises, disciplined folds, or pressure in spots the pool usually avoids.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Assume low-stakes players call too wide, raise too honestly, and underbluff big passive lines until their history proves otherwise.
Trigger question: Before continuing, ask: what hands are actually still consistent with this action, sizing, and player type?
Start with the population prior, then update. Low-stakes players call too wide, raise too honestly, and underbluff big river lines until proven otherwise.
Remove hands street by street. Do not keep weak floats, missed draws, or slowplays in range after later actions make them unlikely.
Treat passive raises as value-heavy. When a passive player raises turn or river, require a specific reason before assigning many bluffs.
advanced_notes: A population read can be disciplined rather than lazy.

The shortcut is not 'all players are the same.' The shortcut is a prior that must be updated. Starting from the pool tendency prevents you from inventing bluffs or slowplays that this opponent has not earned.

chapters.title: Sizing Tells and Bet Construction
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is to treat sizing as both a betting tool and a live read: bets should target customers, and opponent sizes often reveal intent. In plain English: start with name the worse hands that call before value betting; name the folds before bluffing; size up against sticky customers and stop bluffing inelastic callers. and protect yourself from choosing a standard size because it looks normal instead of because it attacks a real calling or folding range.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of bet sizing, value targeting, fold equity, price setting. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Sizing is unusually informative in live pools because many players size for comfort rather than balance. Small bets can set a price, blocker bets can reveal medium strength, and oversized bets from passive players often represent real value rather than a balanced mix.
takeaways: Name the worse hands that call before value betting; name the folds before bluffing; size up against sticky customers and stop bluffing inelastic callers.
Avoid this leak: Choosing a standard size because it looks normal instead of because it attacks a real calling or folding range.
Ask before acting: Before choosing a size, ask: who is the customer for this bet, and what price will that customer actually pay or fold to?
what_to_do_first: Choose size from the customer list. First name the worse hands that call, then pick the largest size those hands can realistically pay.
Go bigger versus sticky callers. If top pair, second pair, or obvious draws will continue, use larger value sizes instead of defaulting small.
Do not bluff the inelastic caller. If a player calls because they like their hand, your elegant sizing story is mostly irrelevant.
Read tiny blocker bets carefully. They often mean medium-strength hands setting price; raise value wider when worse can call and bluff only when folds are realistic.

decision_questions.question: How big should I bet for value?
decision_questions.answer: List the worse hands that call, then choose the largest size those hands realistically pay. Sticky opponents permit larger value sizes.

decision_questions.question: When is a small bet a tell?
decision_questions.answer: Often it is a price-setting or blocker action with medium strength. Raise for value when worse hands can call; bluff only when the player can actually fold.

decision_questions.question: How do I avoid bad bluffs?
decision_questions.answer: Do not bluff an inelastic caller. If the player calls because they like their hand, your elegant size and story lose much of their force.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Name the worse hands that call before value betting; name the folds before bluffing; size up against sticky customers and stop bluffing inelastic callers.
Trigger question: Before choosing a size, ask: who is the customer for this bet, and what price will that customer actually pay or fold to?
Choose size from the customer list. First name the worse hands that call, then pick the largest size those hands can realistically pay.
Go bigger versus sticky callers. If top pair, second pair, or obvious draws will continue, use larger value sizes instead of defaulting small.
Do not bluff the inelastic caller. If a player calls because they like their hand, your elegant sizing story is mostly irrelevant.
advanced_notes: The best size is not always the one that looks balanced.
Against sticky live players, larger value sizes can be better than elegant small bets. Against inelastic callers, bluff sizing theory matters less than whether the player can fold at all.

chapters.title: Thin Value and Getting Paid
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that thin value is a major part of live-cash win rate because loose players call too wide while still raising too honestly. In plain English: start with bet when enough worse hands can call, size for payment, and be willing to bet-fold when a passive opponent's raise changes the information. and protect yourself from checking back profitable value because a rare raise would feel uncomfortable.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of thin value, worse-hand calling range, loose-passive opponents, raise risk. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Thin value works because the same player pool that underbluffs big lines often calls too wide with bluff-catchers, second pair, top pair weak kicker, and hands that feel too pretty to fold. That combination creates a clean exploit: bet more often when worse hands call, but do not pay off raises automatically.
takeaways: Bet when enough worse hands can call, size for payment, and be willing to bet-fold when a passive opponent's raise changes the information.
Avoid this leak: Checking back profitable value because a rare raise would feel uncomfortable.
Ask before acting: Before checking back, ask: which worse hands call if I bet, and how often do I really get punished by a raise?
what_to_do_first: Bet thinly when worse hands call. Top pair weak kicker, second pair, and overpairs can often value bet against loose-passive opponents when raises remain honest.
Use bet-fold as a weapon. Getting called too wide and getting raised too honestly can both be true in the same pool.
Size thin value for payment. Use smaller-to-medium sizes when worse hands are fragile; size up when opponents are sticky or the board improves their bluff-catchers.
Do not check back just to avoid a rare raise. Estimate raise frequency and raise meaning; do not let fear erase profitable value.

decision_questions.question: What counts as thin value?
decision_questions.answer: A bet is thin value when enough worse hands call often enough, even if your hand is not thrilled to face a raise.

decision_questions.question: Can I bet and still fold?
decision_questions.answer: Yes. Low-stakes pools can call too wide and raise too honestly, which makes bet-fold one of the cleanest exploitative lines.

decision_questions.question: How do I size thin value?
decision_questions.answer: Use smaller or medium sizes when worse hands are fragile; size up when opponents are sticky or the runout improves hands that can bluff-catch.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Bet when enough worse hands can call, size for payment, and be willing to bet-fold when a passive opponent's raise changes the information.
Trigger question: Before checking back, ask: which worse hands call if I bet, and how often do I really get punished by a raise?
Bet thinly when worse hands call. Top pair weak kicker, second pair, and overpairs can often value bet against loose-passive opponents when raises remain honest.
Use bet-fold as a weapon. Getting called too wide and getting raised too honestly can both be true in the same pool.
Size thin value for payment. Use smaller-to-medium sizes when worse hands are fragile; size up when opponents are sticky or the board improves their bluff-catchers.
advanced_notes: Bet-fold can be aggressive, not timid.
The pool can make two mistakes at once: call too wide and raise too honestly. Thin value captures the first mistake; folding to the raise respects the second.

chapters.title: River Underbluffing and the Curiosity Tax
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that big passive river aggression is usually value-heavy, so curiosity is not a reason to pay. In plain English: start with fold more against large river bets and raises from players without proven bluff frequency; call wider only when the line contains real missed hands and opponent proof. and protect yourself from converting pot odds into permission while skipping the harder question of whether villain has enough bluffs.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of river underbluffing, bluff catching, pot odds, curiosity tax. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? River mistakes are expensive because the pot is already large and curiosity feels like analysis. In many low-stakes games, passive players do not arrive at big river bets and raises with enough bluffs, especially after taking lines that look value-heavy all the way down.
takeaways: Fold more against large river bets and raises from players without proven bluff frequency; call wider only when the line contains real missed hands and opponent proof.
Avoid this leak: Converting pot odds into permission while skipping the harder question of whether villain has enough bluffs.
Ask before acting: Before calling river, ask: what exact bluffs arrive here this way, and has this opponent shown the nerve to use them?
what_to_do_first: Default fold more to large passive river aggression. The pool usually does not produce enough bluffs in big river bets and raises.
Use pot odds only after bluff frequency. A good price is not enough if the opponent's range is still overwhelmingly value.
Call wider only with proof. Prior shown bluffs, missed draws that fit the line, and aggressive player history can earn a deviation.
Separate value betting from paying off. You can value bet thinly and still fold when raised.

decision_questions.question: Do pot odds force a call?
decision_questions.answer: No. Pot odds only matter after the range has enough bluffs. Against value-heavy live lines, a cheap call can still be a losing call.

decision_questions.question: When do I hero call?
decision_questions.answer: Hero call when missed draws fit the line, the player has shown bluffing nerve, and your hand blocks value or unblocks bluffs. Do not call just to see.

decision_questions.question: Can I value bet thinly and fold to a raise?
decision_questions.answer: Yes. Those are compatible exploits: get paid by loose calls, then respect honest raises.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Fold more against large river bets and raises from players without proven bluff frequency; call wider only when the line contains real missed hands and opponent proof.
Trigger question: Before calling river, ask: what exact bluffs arrive here this way, and has this opponent shown the nerve to use them?
Default fold more to large passive river aggression. The pool usually does not produce enough bluffs in big river bets and raises.
Use pot odds only after bluff frequency. A good price is not enough if the opponent's range is still overwhelmingly value.
Call wider only with proof. Prior shown bluffs, missed draws that fit the line, and aggressive player history can earn a deviation.
advanced_notes: A mathematically cheap call can still be a bad call.
Pot odds do not create bluffs in the opponent's range. If the line is value-heavy and the player lacks bluff history, the cheap price may simply make a losing call look disciplined.

chapters.title: Bluffing Where the Pool Actually Folds
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is not to bluff more; it is to bluff where ranges are capped, players can fold, and your story threatens believable future pressure. In plain English: start with bluff heads-up against capped ranges and fold-capable opponents; give up against sticky players, multiway fields, and runouts that improve the calling range. and protect yourself from using sophisticated blocker language to justify pressure against people who came to call.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of fold equity, blockers, capped ranges, credible story. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Bluffing is profitable in live cash when the target is right, not when the story is clever. Players fold most often when their range is capped, the pot is heads-up, the board pressures their bluff-catchers, and the opponent is actually capable of releasing a hand.
takeaways: Bluff heads-up against capped ranges and fold-capable opponents; give up against sticky players, multiway fields, and runouts that improve the calling range.
Avoid this leak: Using sophisticated blocker language to justify pressure against people who came to call.
Ask before acting: Before bluffing, ask: what better hands fold, and why would this opponent release them today?
what_to_do_first: Bluff capped ranges, not sticky personalities. The best bluff target is a range with too few strong hands held by a player capable of folding.
Prefer heads-up pressure. Multiway pots dramatically reduce fold equity in low-stakes live games.
Use blockers as confirmation, not permission. A blocker improves a bluff that already has fold target, credible story, and player fit.
Give up when the turn changes the customer. If villain's continuing range improves or becomes inelastic, stop telling the story.

decision_questions.question: Who should I bluff?
decision_questions.answer: Bluff players who can fold and arrive with capped ranges. Do not bluff the player whose main mistake is curiosity calling.

decision_questions.question: When do blockers matter?
decision_questions.answer: After the target, story, and fold equity already make sense. Blockers improve a good bluff; they do not rescue a bad one.

decision_questions.question: Why avoid multiway bluffs?
decision_questions.answer: Each extra caller lowers fold equity and adds someone who connected enough to continue. Low-stakes multiway pots are usually value territory.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Bluff heads-up against capped ranges and fold-capable opponents; give up against sticky players, multiway fields, and runouts that improve the calling range.
Trigger question: Before bluffing, ask: what better hands fold, and why would this opponent release them today?
Bluff capped ranges, not sticky personalities. The best bluff target is a range with too few strong hands held by a player capable of folding.
Prefer heads-up pressure. Multiway pots dramatically reduce fold equity in low-stakes live games.
Use blockers as confirmation, not permission. A blocker improves a bluff that already has fold target, credible story, and player fit.
advanced_notes: Good bluffing can mean bluffing less often overall.
The goal is not to prove courage. It is to concentrate pressure where folds actually happen: capped ranges, heads-up pots, credible runouts, and opponents with a fold button.

chapters.title: Ego, Emotion, and Table Discipline
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that emotional discipline is a technical skill because ego changes calling, bluffing, and quitting thresholds. In plain English: start with pause before expensive nodes, name the emotional trigger, and return to the source-backed default unless evidence justifies a deviation. and protect yourself from disguising image repair, revenge, boredom, or embarrassment as strategy.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of tilt, ego protection, decision fatigue, session discipline. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Emotional mistakes work the same way as technical leaks: they appear in repeatable nodes. A player calls because they hate being bluffed, bets because they want to punish someone, or keeps playing because quitting would feel like admitting defeat.
takeaways: Pause before expensive nodes, name the emotional trigger, and return to the source-backed default unless evidence justifies a deviation.
Avoid this leak: Disguising image repair, revenge, boredom, or embarrassment as strategy.
Ask before acting: Before acting fast, ask: am I trying to maximize EV or change how this hand makes me feel?
what_to_do_first: Install a pause before big calls and big bluffs. If the decision feels personal, slow down before chips move.
Fold when the motive is image repair. Calling to avoid being bluffed or betting to punish someone is not an EV reason.
Quit before discipline collapses. Fatigue lowers bluff thresholds, widens calls, and makes bad evidence feel convincing.
Track emotional triggers as leaks. Mark hands where the reason was curiosity, revenge, boredom, or embarrassment.

decision_questions.question: What is the fastest emotional leak to catch?
decision_questions.answer: Rushed action. Speed often means the decision is protecting ego, avoiding discomfort, or chasing a feeling.

decision_questions.question: How do I stop hero-call tilt?
decision_questions.answer: Name the motive. If the reason is not range, price, blocker, or opponent history, fold and write the spot down for review.

decision_questions.question: When should I quit?
decision_questions.answer: Quit or take a real break when fatigue changes your thresholds: wider calls, punitive bluffs, impatience, or a desire to win back identity rather than chips.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Pause before expensive nodes, name the emotional trigger, and return to the source-backed default unless evidence justifies a deviation.
Trigger question: Before acting fast, ask: am I trying to maximize EV or change how this hand makes me feel?
Install a pause before big calls and big bluffs. If the decision feels personal, slow down before chips move.
Fold when the motive is image repair. Calling to avoid being bluffed or betting to punish someone is not an EV reason.
Quit before discipline collapses. Fatigue lowers bluff thresholds, widens calls, and makes bad evidence feel convincing.
advanced_notes: The emotional fold is sometimes the professional play.
Folding when you feel challenged can look weak in the moment, but it protects the bankroll when the call is really about identity rather than range, price, and player history.

chapters.title: The Session Review and Improvement Loop
chapters.plain_english_summary: The central lesson is that improvement comes from capturing decisions clearly, grouping them by leak family, and drilling the most frequent expensive patterns first. In plain English: start with record position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, and your reason; review by decision family rather than by whether the pot was won. and protect yourself from reviewing only dramatic hands while the repeated small leak keeps billing every session.
chapters.beginner_explanation: This chapter uses the ideas of session review, leak tracking, hand selection, feedback loop. If those words are new, treat them as labels for one practical question: what information do you have, what mistake is the table making, and which action earns money from that mistake? Review works when it preserves the decision before memory rewrites it. Low-stakes players often remember the result, the bad beat, or the embarrassment, but forget the position, stack depth, sizing, and player type that actually determine whether the line was good.
takeaways: Record position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, and your reason; review by decision family rather than by whether the pot was won.
Avoid this leak: Reviewing only dramatic hands while the repeated small leak keeps billing every session.
Ask before acting: After a session, ask: which mistake repeated, how much did it cost, and what trigger will stop it next time?
what_to_do_first: Record the decision, not just the result. Capture position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, and your reason before memory cleans it up.
Bucket hands by leak family. Preflop discipline, thin value, river bluff-catching, sizing tells, and tilt are more useful categories than won/lost.
Fix frequent expensive leaks first. A common $60 mistake beats a rare $600 cooler as a study priority.
Build one table trigger per leak. The review should produce a sentence you can remember mid-hand.

decision_questions.question: What should I write down?
decision_questions.answer: Record the decision ingredients: position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, board, your reason, and the result only after the reason is captured.

decision_questions.question: How should I group hands?
decision_questions.answer: Bucket by leak family: preflop discipline, thin value, river calls, bluff selection, sizing tells, or emotional triggers. Won/lost is not the study category.

decision_questions.question: What should I fix first?
decision_questions.answer: Fix the leak that is frequent, expensive, and recognizable in real time. A repeatable trigger beats a vague promise to play better.

rules_of_thumb: Default: Record position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, and your reason; review by decision family rather than by whether the pot was won.
Trigger question: After a session, ask: which mistake repeated, how much did it cost, and what trigger will stop it next time?
Record the decision, not just the result. Capture position, stack depth, player type, action, sizing, and your reason before memory cleans it up.
Bucket hands by leak family. Preflop discipline, thin value, river bluff-catching, sizing tells, and tilt are more useful categories than won/lost.
Fix frequent expensive leaks first. A common $60 mistake beats a rare $600 cooler as a study priority.
advanced_notes: The most useful hand to study may be the ordinary one you keep misplaying.
Rare coolers are memorable, but frequent repeatable leaks are more valuable study targets. The repair loop should prioritize mistakes you can recognize and stop next session.

glossary.definition: Where you act in the betting order.
glossary.beginner_note: Acting later is powerful because you see what opponents do before deciding.

glossary.definition: The betting lead from the previous aggressive action.
glossary.beginner_note: Initiative helps only when it clarifies future streets.

glossary.definition: A range with too few very strong hands.
glossary.beginner_note: Capped ranges are better bluff targets because they struggle to call big pressure.

glossary.definition: A card in your hand that makes some opponent value hands less likely.
glossary.beginner_note: Blockers support a bluff; they do not justify one by themselves.

glossary.definition: A value bet with a hand that is ahead often enough but not invulnerable.
glossary.beginner_note: Thin value is how live players get paid by worse one-pair hands.

glossary.definition: The price you are getting to call compared with the pot.
glossary.beginner_note: Pot odds matter only after the opponent has enough bluffs.

glossary.definition: A spot where opponents bluff less often than theory or the price requires.
glossary.beginner_note: Big passive river aggression is often underbluffed in low-stakes live games.

glossary.definition: A raise against limpers intended to play heads-up or in position.
glossary.beginner_note: Isolation works best when worse hands call and you act after them.

glossary.definition: The set of hands an opponent can plausibly have.
glossary.beginner_note: Do not keep every preflop hand in range after later actions contradict it.

glossary.definition: Betting for value with the plan to fold if raised.
glossary.beginner_note: This works when the pool calls too wide but raises too honestly.

visual_scenarios.title: Value-first default in a loose live game
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Low-stakes poker table with chips and open felt for lesson overlays.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: $1/$3, loose caller defends blind, river decision after two value bets.

action_steps.action: Hero raises button; loose blind calls.
action_steps.lesson: Enter with position and a hand that can make clean top pair.

action_steps.action: Hero bets top pair for value.
action_steps.lesson: The customer is worse queens, eights, and sticky floats.

action_steps.action: Hero value bets again unless raised.
action_steps.lesson: Value first; do not invent fear without evidence.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Low-stakes profit starts by identifying who will pay with worse hands.

visual_scenarios.title: Isolating limpers from position
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Preflop live poker table with limpers and a player reaching for a raise.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: $1/$3, two loose limpers, blinds passive, stacks around $400.

action_steps.action: Two players limp; hero raises to 5bb plus 1bb per limper.
action_steps.lesson: Raise larger because live callers continue too wide.

action_steps.action: Proceed mostly in position against dominated broadways and suited trash.
action_steps.lesson: The raise is a value-and-position tool, not automatic aggression.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Isolate when your hand is ahead of limp-calling ranges and position lets you realize the edge.

visual_scenarios.title: Betting flop only with a turn plan
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Poker table scene used for a position and initiative lesson.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Heads-up after hero opens button and small blind calls.

action_steps.action: Hero continuation bets a dry king-high board.
action_steps.lesson: The board favors hero's range and villain has many weak continues.

action_steps.action: Continue on spades, queens, aces, and broadway pressure cards.
action_steps.lesson: Initiative is useful because future decisions are named in advance.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Position plus initiative earns money when the next street is planned before chips go in.

visual_scenarios.title: Respecting a passive turn raise
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Heads-up poker pressure scene for a range-reading lesson.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Hero value bets flop and turn; passive player check-raises turn.

action_steps.action: Villain check-calls.
action_steps.lesson: Range remains wide: pairs, draws, slowplays.

action_steps.action: Villain check-raises.
action_steps.lesson: Passive raises remove many weak calls and weight range toward value.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Start with the population prior, then narrow the range when the line becomes honest.

visual_scenarios.title: Choosing size from the customer list
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Poker table value-bet scene with chips sliding forward.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: River after villain has check-called twice.

action_steps.action: Name worse callers: Qx, 8x, stubborn pocket pairs.
action_steps.lesson: Value sizing starts with customers.

action_steps.action: Hero chooses a size those worse hands can pay.
action_steps.lesson: A standard size is less important than the calling range.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Pick the biggest size that worse hands realistically call.

visual_scenarios.title: Thin value with a bet-fold plan
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Thin-value river bet scene at a live poker table.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Loose opponent calls too many one-pair hands but raises honestly.

action_steps.action: Hero bets top pair for thin value.
action_steps.lesson: Enough worse tens and sevens can call.

action_steps.action: Hero folds to a passive raise.
action_steps.lesson: Calling too wide and raising too honestly can both be true.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Thin value and disciplined folding are compatible exploits.

visual_scenarios.title: The curiosity-tax river call
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Tense river call decision at a live poker table.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Villain bets large on river after passive line suddenly polarizes.

action_steps.action: Hero is getting a tempting price.
action_steps.lesson: Price is not enough by itself.

action_steps.action: Ask what exact missed hands bluff this size.
action_steps.lesson: If the list is imaginary, fold.

visual_scenarios.lesson: A call is earned by real bluff frequency, not by wanting to see it.

visual_scenarios.title: Bluffing a capped range heads-up
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Villain check-calls flop and turn, then checks river on a pressure card.

action_steps.action: Villain rarely has sets or strong two pair.
action_steps.lesson: The range is capped enough to pressure.

action_steps.action: Hero blocks some ace-high continues and tells a credible story.
action_steps.lesson: Blockers confirm; they do not create the bluff alone.

visual_scenarios.lesson: Bluff where better hands can actually fold.

visual_scenarios.title: The ten-second ego reset
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Calm poker mental-game reset scene with chips and notebook.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: Opponent applies pressure after showing a bluff earlier.

action_steps.action: Hero pauses and names the motive.
action_steps.lesson: If the motive is image repair, it is not evidence.

action_steps.action: Hero returns to range, price, and opponent history.
action_steps.lesson: Emotion becomes a signal, not an instruction.

visual_scenarios.lesson: A pause before expensive nodes protects technical judgment.

visual_scenarios.title: Reviewing the decision, not the result
visual_scenarios.alt_text: Poker study desk with notebook, chips, and laptop edge.
visual_scenarios.pot_context: A river value-check spot is logged after the session.

action_steps.action: Write position, stack depth, action, sizing, player type, and reason.
action_steps.lesson: Record the decision before memory edits it.

action_steps.action: Bucket the hand as missed thin value.
action_steps.lesson: Fix frequent, recognizable leaks first.

visual_scenarios.lesson: The review loop turns a hand into a table trigger for next session.
